EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching occlusion from the gulf of Alaska produced Heavy Snow over the crest area of the Eastern Alaska Range. A spotter at Summit Lake reported 12.0 inches of snowfall during the event, which only lasted around 8 hours.

An occlusion movced north from the Alaska Peninsula on the evening of the 27th toward the north Bering Sea, causing increasing winds and blowing snow. Blizzard conditions likely occurred at Gambell, as reported by the AWOS there.

With very cold air over the North Slope, east winds increased to 15 to 25 mph on the 22nd over the Western Arctic Coast, creating Extreme Wind Chills until clouds moved in during late morning of the 23rd, raising temperatures. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm moved into the Bering Sea as a second low associated with this storm moved into the Gulf of Alaska. The resulting strong west wind flow produced a storm surge along the Bristol Bay coast during the high tide that resulted in flooding in the community of Pilot Point.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: People in Pilot Point reported very large waves, described as impressive, moving in with the incoming high tide. Flooding occurred up to the old cannery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm moved into the eastern Aleutians Saturday afternoon and moved to the Alaska Peninsula Saturday night. Strong wind and snow produced blizzard conditions in the eastern Aleutians and Alaska Peninsula as the storm moved through the region.

A strong low in the Bering Sea and the associated front produced strong wind through the mountain gaps of the Kenai Peninsula. The peak gusts were 85 mph along the hillside and 76 mph in Portage Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An occlusion moving northeast over the Bering Sea brought strong winds and blizzard conditions to portions of the Bering Strait Coast and Saint Lawrence Island. Blizzard conditions were likely at Savoonga, as reported by the AWOS there. Peak wind gusts briefly reached 59 mph, just 1 mph short of warning criteria.

An upper low circulation moved south over the Arctic Ocean toward the Central Beaufort Sea Coast on the 6th and the circulation produced heavy snow at the Cooperative Observer Station at Colville River. Snow began at 0800 AST on the 6th. Snow ended at 1530 AST on the 7th with 8.6 inches total accumulation. Winds were light so there was no drifting of snow. Heavy Snow criteria was estimated to be met at 0500 AST on the 7th.

CZ_NAME: SALISBURY SOUND TO CAPE FAIRWEATHER COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The arctic front stalled over the southern-most part of the SE Alaska Panhandle on the night of Saturday 3/3 and remained stationary for a couple of days. On Sunday afternoon, 3/4, a Low developed off the Queen Charlotte Islands sending warm moist air aloft over the arctic front through Monday, 3/5, afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pelican CO-OP measured 8 inches of new snowfall for 24 hours ending at 0700 AKST 3/6. Elfin cove A-Paid/CO-OP measured 12.5 inches new snowfall midnight to midnight on 3/5.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weather front moving north over western Alaska produced a period of heavy snowfall at the village of Nulato. The Cooperative Observer reported  a total of 16 inches of snowfall. Snow began around 2300AST on the 5th, and ended at 1700ast on the 6th.  The snowfall is estimated to have reached warning criteria of 8 inches at 0800AST on the 6th.

A weather front moving north over western Alaska caused high winds to occur in Windy Pass. The State of Alaska Roadway Weather Information System platform at Antler Creek recorded a maximum gust of 63 knots (73 mph).

With a cold airmass in place, a weak trough developed inland from the Arctic Coast resulting in winds speeds of 15 mph and creating cold Wind Chills, as reported by the Deadhorse ASOS; the lowest wind chill was 68 F degrees below zero during the period.
An intense low moved across the Aleutians in the 3rd and 4th of January. This storm produce snow and strong wind across most of the western Aleutian islands.
Strong cold air advection behind a low to the east of the Pribilof Islands caused northerly gusts and snow showers that reduced visibilities to a quarter mile or less at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong cold air advection behind a low to the east of the Pribilof Islands caused northerly gusts to 45 mph and snow showers that resulted in blizzard conditions for about four hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm in the north Pacific and its associated weather front caused gusty south winds, snow, and blowing snow across southwest Alaska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds peaked at 56 mph along the coast with visibilities reduced to near zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm in the north Pacific and its associated weather front caused gusty south winds, snow, and blowing snow across southwest Alaska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong southerly winds produced blizzard conditions along the western capes of Bristol Bay. Visibility was reduced to near zero at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weather front in the western Gulf of Alaska caused an upslope event north and west of Talkeetna in the Susitna Valley. Snow totals ranged from 18 to 24 inches across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter network reports ranged around 18 inches of snow by the morning of the 11th with snow still continuing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm moved into the western Aleutians producing snow and strong wind across the Aleutian Islands. Blizzard conditions occurred over the western Aleutians.
Strong northerly winds through Thompson Pass created a ground blizzard that dropped visibilities to near zero.

A strong low moved into the Gulf of Alaska on January 20th. Snow moved into Kodiak Island and Prince William Sound as the low approached. In advance of the front associated with this storm strong wind blew across Kodiak Island and Prince William sound creating blizzard conditions across Kodiak Island and in northern Prince William Sound in Thompson Pass.

A strong low moved into the Gulf of Alaska on January 20th. Snow moved into Kodiak Island and Prince William Sound as the low approached. In advance of the front associated with this storm strong wind blew across Kodiak Island and Prince William sound creating blizzard conditions across Kodiak Island and in northern Prince William Sound in Thompson Pass.

A low over the central Bering Sea and another low near Kodiak Island helped to produce strong winds over western Alaska. A weather front from the Bering Sea low moved north over Eastern Norton sound and briefly produced high winds at Unalakleet, where the AWOS reported a maximum gust of 53 knots (61 mph).
A strong front moved inland along the North Gulf Coast New Years Eve producing snow and strong wind through Thompson Pass resulting in a Blizzard.

A strong low moved over northwest Prince William Sound through the day January 3rd. this storm produced areas of heavy snow along with strong northwest wind across the southern Kenai Peninsula. Blizzard conditions developed over the southern Kenai Peninsula, particularly in the Kachemak Bay and Homer area.
An intense low moved across the Aleutians in the 3rd and 4th of January. This storm produce snow and strong wind across most of the western Aleutian islands.
A strong low in the Gulf of Alaska produced snow and strong wind in Portage Valley resulting in a blizzard.
An intense north pacific storm moved to the central Aleutians January 15th. this storm produced snow and strong wind resulting in a blizzard across the Pribilof Islands.

Although there was no significant synoptic scale weather system, airmass convection broke out over Southern SE Alaska early on the morning of Tuesday, July 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 0400 AKST on Tuesday July 31st, the crew of the cruise ship STATENDAM observed and photographed a funnel cloud in Felice Strait on the East side of Metlakatla Island (near WSO Annette). No damage occurred and apparently did last only a short period of time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moved through the Kuskokwim Delta possibly producing  large hail and strong gusty wind near 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The severity of this storm is estimated from Radar returns and the magnitude of wind observed near the thunderstorm. While the actual observed wind was not 60 mph, it is reasonable to assume the wind did reach 60 mph due to the sparse data network in this region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm moved into the western Aleutians and Bering Sea September 17th and 18th. As the storm crossed the Aleutians, mariners reported wind gusts to 90 mph.
An intense North Pacific low moved across the Aleutians into the Bering Sea September 10th. This produced the typically strong wind in the Eastern Aleutians.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dutch Harbor Harbor Master estimated wind gusts near 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm that formed along the Alaska Range blew up into a severe thunderstorm producing 1 inch hail as it moved through Paxson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail size for this storm is estimated based upon storms within the same complex that had hail up to 1 inch in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed on the interior Kenai Peninsula. Spotter reports were of hail 1/2 to 3/4 inch and heavy rain with these Thunderstorms.
Severe thunderstorms developed on the interior Kenai Peninsula. Spotter reports were of hail 1/2 to 3/4 inch and heavy rain with these Thunderstorms.

A strong low pushed a front into the Eastern Bering Sea producing strong east wind along with snow across the Pribilof Islands. Blizzard conditions began late on the afternoon of the 19th and persisted through the afternoon of the 21st.
A storm moved toward Prince william Sound generating strong wind and snow in the western Sound.

A low pressure system moved into Prince William Sound bringing snow to the eastern Kenai Peninsula. A moderately strong pressure gradient on the west side of the low caused gusty winds especially in and below mountain passes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusts to 40 mph reduced visibility to a quarter mile at times along the eastern Kenai Peninsula.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved into Prince William Sound bringing snow to the eastern Kenai Peninsula. A moderately strong pressure gradient on the west side of the low caused gusty winds especially in and below mountain passes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A moderately strong pressure gradient on the north and west side of the low in Prince William Sound caused gusty winds especially in and below mountain passes. Gusts to 54 mph reduced visibility in Thompson Pass to a quarter mile or less.|

An intense north Pacific storm moved to the central Aleutians with a secondary storm center south of the Alaska Peninsula. High Wind swept through southwest and south central Alaska and along the central Aleutians and Alaska Peninsula. Snow over the central Aleutians combined with the wind resulted in a Blizzard for that region. Wide spread power outages plagued the Kuskokwim Delat with this storm along with roofs being blown off two houses, two housed shifted on their foundation and minor tidal overflow along Kuskokwim Bay. Unconfirmed wind gusts were reported to 127 mph at Sand Point on the Alaska Peninsula with this storm.

High pressure north of the state and low pressure centers near Kodiak Island and over the Bering Sea created a strong pressure gradient and caused high winds to occur on the night of the 3rd and morning of the 4th at Kaktovik. The Barter Island AWOS reported east-southeast winds gusting to 67 knots (77 mph) at 0215AST on the 4th. The highest sustained wind reported at this location was 55 knots (63 mph) at 0515AST.

Strong high pressure northeast of the state and a low pressure center traveling north over the Bering Sea, caused high winds to occur at Barter Island. The pressure gradient and winds relaxed quickly as the low weakened, the high slid eastward, and a surface trough over the Arctic slope progressed northward. At the Barter Island AWOS, the highest gust was 53 knots (61 mph).

Southerly flow aloft and at low levels caused and upslope snowstorm on 11/16.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP Observer at Haines Customs (HCNA2) observed 9.5 inches of new snow ending at 0700 AKST 11/26. The observer commented that the new snowfall was overnight and had become light at 0700.

CZ_NAME: DIXON ENTRANCE TO CAPE DECISION COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 985 MB Low in the Western Gulf of Alaska weakened to 991 MB near Middleton Island early on the morning of Friday, November 2nd. A strong front moved onto Prince of Wales Island around 0600 AKST 11/2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cooperative observer at Craig observed a wind gust of 64 MPH at 0600 on November second.

CZ_NAME: DIXON ENTRANCE TO CAPE DECISION COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Storm Force Low rapidly developed to 996 MB just off Prince of Wales Island early on Monday morning, 11/5 with extreme surface pressure gradients.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer at Craig recorded at wind gust of 76 MPH at 0316 AKST on|November fifth.

CZ_NAME: JUNEAU BOROUGH AND NORTHERN ADMIRALTY ISLAND
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Saint Patrick's Day was white instead of green for much of the Northern Panhandle in 2007. A weak Low formed on the arctic front and developed near Cape Spencer Friday Afternoon 3/16. This secondary Low moved very slowly inland throughout Saturday, 3/17 while the main upper and surface  Low remained over the Western Gulf.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observation of 8.3 inches of new snowfall was taken at the Juneau office for the 12 hours before 0443 AKST on March seventeenth. Juneau Airport measured a storm total of 7.2 inches for this event.

CZ_NAME: DIXON ENTRANCE TO CAPE DECISION COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm force low developed in the Western Gulf of Alaska on Thursday, 4/6. A secondary low developed on the front that had moved into the Eastern Gulf Thursday night becoming 970 MB off the Queen Charlotte Islands. This second low recurved back into the Central Gulf on Friday afternoon with a very strong front moving onto the SE Alaska Coast. This storm rapidly weakened in the western Gulf late on Friday night 4/6.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hydaburg had a measured gust of 60 MPH at 1256 on April sixth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm force low rapidly developed to the southwest with a very strong frontal system on the afternoon of Saturday, January 6th. The Low center made landfall near Cape Decision Saturday evening at 979 MB. Very strong surface pressure gradients were ahead of this Storm. Warm air was able to overrun along the northern quadrants of this Low for heavy snow Sunday morning. A secondary Low developed in the Central Gulf pushing more warm, moist air over the arctic front Sunday afternoon and evening, Jan. 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Annette had a peak wind of 49 knots at 2353 on January sixth. Gusts to 47 knots occurred at Hydaburg at 2216.

CZ_NAME: DIXON ENTRANCE TO CAPE DECISION COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front along 135W on Sunday, Jan. 14th, moved slowly across SE Alaska from the west on Monday, Jan. 15th. On the 16th the arctic front was parked over Lynn Canal with some warm air overruning ahead of the next system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hydaburg observed a gust to 52 knots 0016 on January fifteenth.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Annette observed a measured gust to 51 knots at 2353 on January fourteenth.

CZ_NAME: SALISBURY SOUND TO CAPE FAIRWEATHER COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While the arctic front remained stationary over over the Southern Panhandle on 3/5 and 3/6, a 967 MB storm force Low moved into the Eastern Gulf on Tuesday 3/6. This pattern, which had been so persistent during this winter season, was again repeated in that warm moist air was forced aloft above the arctic front through the early Wednesday morning, 3/7.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cape Spencer observed a measured gust of 60 MPH at 2323 on March the sixth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex Low over the entire Gulf on Tuesday, 3/27, redeveloped to Storm Force 996 MB just off Cape Decision early Wednesday morning 3/28. This system brought high winds to Southern SE Alaska Tuesday night into Wed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low in the north Pacific Ocean and the its associated front moved westward just south of the central Aleutian Islands.  The gust of 102 mph reported at the Dutch Harbor Handar is suspisious as nearby sites were only peaking around 70 mph.

A 1034 MB high about 600 miles north of Prudhoe Bay and a low pressure center moving north over western Alaska tightened the pressure gradient over the Arctic Coast. A small but very significant trough of low pressure just inland from the Arctic Coast strengthened at the beginning of the episode to contribute to the tight gradient, as well as acceleration of the wind under a low level inversion. A short wave feature on the second day of high winds enhanced the gradient and prolonged the high winds. ||Zone 203: Deadhorse ASOS reached a peak wind of 52 knots (60 mph) at 0913AST.|Zone 204: Barter Island AWOS reported the highest gust as 59 knots (68 mph) and the highest sustained wind of 52 knots (60 mph) at 0955AST 11/24. The gust was the last of several occurrences over a 4-hour period.

A warm front moving north from Bristol Bay produced heavy snow over the southeast slopes of the Nulato Hills including the village of Kaltag during the 4th. The Cooperative Observer at Kaltag reported 8 inches of snow by 1900AST on the 5th. Light snow continued intermittently at Kaltag thereafter.

Areas of low pressure over the western Bering Sea and the Northwest Territory of Canada combined with a persistent high over the Arctic Ocean  created blizzard conditions over a portion of the zone, as reported by the weather observer at Kuparuk. East winds gusted up to 35 knots (40 mph).

CZ_NAME: N. BROOKS RNG E OF COLVILLE R
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An occlusion moved over interior Alaska and caused sustained winds to increase enough through Anaktuvuk Pass to create Extreme Wind Chills.  The lowest Wind Chill value reported, calculated from the Anaktuvuk Pass AWOS, was 62 degrees below zero.

The arctic front had become stationary over northern southeast Alaska on Monday, 12/24 while a storm force low center had moved into the western Gulf that afternoon. A series of fronts slowly moved onshore from this low causing warm, moist air to move over the arctic front on Tuesday, 12/25.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Haines customs observed 9.0 inches of new snowfall for 24 hours ending 0800 on 12/25. Analyses showed that most, if not all of the snow fell between 0300 and 0800 on Tuesday, 12/25.

CZ_NAME: CAPE DECISION TO SALISBURY SOUND COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of 12/31 a storm was developing well southwest of the Panhandle. Although this system remained nearly stationary, extreme surface pressure gradients developed against arctic high pressure over the Yukon during new year's eve. The storm weakened rapidly later on new year's day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sitka observed a measured gust of 48 MPH at 2200 on 12/31. Analyses showed extreme surface pressure gradients on 12/31 and 1/1/08. A marine observation at Cape Decision had a measured gust of 63 knots at 2200 on new year's eve.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm moved into the eastern Aleutians Saturday afternoon and moved to the Alaska Peninsula Saturday night. Strong wind and snow produced blizzard conditions in the eastern Aleutians and Alaska Peninsula as the storm moved through the region.
A strong low moved into Prince William Sound producing strong wind and snow resulting in a Blizzard.
A strong low moved into Prince William Sound producing strong wind and snow resulting in a Blizzard.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A volunteer weather spotter report wind gusting to near 80 mph.

CZ_NAME: DIXON ENTRANCE TO CAPE DECISION COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On 12/21, the arctic front remained stationary over central southeast Alaska. A 972 MB storm center was approaching from the southwest, and moved onto the south coast by early morning 12/22 and totally dissipated by the afternoon. On Saturday night, a 955 MB storm center had developed  to the southwest of the first one and moved to just off Sitka by Sunday afternoon, finally dissipating over the southern Panhandle on the Sunday night, 12/23.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Craig cooperative observer reported very windy conditions all day, 12/21. An observation at Hydaburg had measured gusts to 58 MPH at 1636 on 12/21.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure in Bristol Bay pushed warm moist air over the Chugach Mountains and into the Copper River Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight inches of snow fell during the early morning hours of the 14th through 0730L. A total of 17.1 inches of snow was received, with the bulk of the snow falling between 0800L and 2000L on the 14th.

CZ_NAME: JUNEAU BOROUGH AND NORTHERN ADMIRALTY ISLAND
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A blocking pattern occurred for Southeast Alaska in early December. A strong arctic High cell built over central Alaska on Sunday, 12/2 and moved slowly southeastward through Thursday, 12/6. The arctic front remained south of the Panhandle through the period and strong outflow winds occurred due to extreme surface pressure gradients north of the front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Taku wind event occurred on 12/2 and 12/3. The South Douglas anemometer recorded many peak wind speeds ranging from 58 miles per hour to 74 miles per hour. Temperatures during this event averaged around 8 degrees Fahrenheit. Freezing spray was reported in the Douglas harbor on 12/3.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On 12/21, the arctic front remained stationary over central southeast Alaska. A 972 MB storm center was approaching from the southwest, and moved onto the south coast by early morning 12/22 and totally dissipated by the afternoon. On Saturday night, a 955 MB storm center had developed  to the southwest of the first one and moved to just off Sitka by Sunday afternoon, finally dissipating over the southern Panhandle on the Sunday night, 12/23.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Annette observed a measured gust to 53 MPH 0553 on 12/22. Analyses indicated that gusts to 70+ mph were common in the area of Port Chester as per local knowledge of the Annette office.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A gale force low was stationary in the western Gulf on 12/10. A moderate front moved slowly onto the coast on the evening of the tenth through the eleventh with warm over running air while it remained cold near the surface at Hyder.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cooperative observer at Hyder observed 7.0 inches of new snow for the twenty-four hours ending at 0800 on the eleventh and 10.5 inches of new snow for the twenty-four hours ending at 0800 on the twelfth. Analyses indicated that most of the 17.5 inches fell from 0300 on the eleventh to |0300 on the twelfth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy over-running snowfall occurred for the Ketchikan area on 12/19 as a moderate strength low moved into the Queen Charlotte Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous spotters reported 6 to 12 inches of new snow across the Ketchikan area on 12/19. This snowstorm required school closures and caused minor, temporary power outages to the Ketchikan area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of 12/31 a storm was developing well southwest of the Panhandle. Although this system remained nearly stationary, extreme surface pressure gradients developed against arctic high pressure over the Yukon during new year's eve. The storm weakened rapidly later on new year's day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Craig reported very windy with higher gusts from 2000 on new year's eve  through the morning of 1/1/08. A marine observation at Cape Decision had a measured gust of 63 knots at 2200 on new year's eve. Hydaburg observed a gust of 51 knots around midnight on new year's eve.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moderate strength low pressure complex remained stationary in the Gulf of Alaska from the 12/15 to 12/17. Warm air was moving over cold air at the surface on the east side of the low centers which affected Zones 17 and 19. Upslope conditions were focused in the Haines area. Temperatures warmed at the surface on the seventeenth, and new snow near sea level was compacted by rain on that date.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Haines Customs observed 7.0 inches of new snow for 24 hours ending 0700 on 12/15. Analyses indicated that all of this new snow fell on the night of 12/14. The snow was rapidly compacted by rain early on the morning of 12/15.

On Saturday night, a 955 MB storm center had developed  to the southwest of the first one and moved to just off Sitka by Sunday afternoon, finally dissipating over the southern Panhandle on the Sunday night, 12/23.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Skagway power had 6 inches  of new snowfall in 12 hours ending at 0900 on 12/22.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, cold high pressure center was positioned over the Interior and strong low pressure was centered over the northeastern Gulf of Alaska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gust to 71 kts (82 mph) recorded on Valdez HANDAR at the Port of Valdez at 0700 AST 2 Dec 07.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough of low pressure moved across lower Cook Inlet overnight producing locally heavy snow along Dimond Ridge near Homer. A spotter along Dimond Ridge reported 12 to 14 inches of snow that began after midnight and had fallen by 8 AM AST.
By Saturday Afternoon, Jan. 20th, a huge storm over the entire Gulf of Alaska had developed to 960 MB. The strong associated front moved onto the SE Alaska Coast early Sunday Morning, Jan. 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Haines Customs (Elevation 820 FT) CO-OP measured 14.0 inches of new snowfall for 24 hours ending 0700 AKST on Sunday, 1/21. Analyses indicate that this entire snow event was during the previous Saturday Night.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Storm moved across the westrn Aleutians while pushing its front to south of the Pribilof Islands Monday March 19th. Strong wind and snow produced a blizzard in the Pribilof Islands.

CZ_NAME: CAPE FAIRWEATHER TO CAPE SUCKLING COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By Saturday Afternoon, Jan. 20th, a huge storm over the entire Gulf of Alaska had developed to 960 MB. The strong associated front moved onto the SE Alaska Coast early Sunday Morning, Jan. 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Yakutat NWS WSO ASOS (PAYA) had a peak wind of 53 KT at 2103 AKST and a measured a gust to 46 KT at 2253 AKST 1/20. It is well known that wind speeds are at least 10 MPH higher along the beach and in outflow regions of the local rivers and Yakutat Bay. Estimated wind speeds were as high as 65 MPH during this storm. |

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The arctic front persisted over the Central Panhandle on Tuesday, January 23rd. A series of weak Lows over the Southern and Central Gulf of Alaska continued to push warm air over the arctic front.  Upslope conditions enhanced the snow in the Northern Panhandle. Cold air was trapped in the Misty Fjords near Hyder at this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Haines Customs CO-OP had 24 hour new snow measured totals of 1/23 Trace, 1/24 9.0 inches, 1/25 2.5 inches. All measurments were made a 0700 AKST. Another spotter in Haines measured 18.0 inches of new snow for 24 hours ending at 0730 1/24, with 24 inches storm total.

CZ_NAME: CAPE FAIRWEATHER TO CAPE SUCKLING COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A gale force Low developed in the Western Gulf on Thursday, Jan. 4th. This main Low stalled in the Central Gulf while the associated strong fronts moved onto the SE Alaska Coast Thursday Night. These fronts did not significantly erode the cold air that was over the Panhandle. The result was that heavy snow occurred on Thursday Night and Friday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS WFO Yakutat (PAYA) measured 10.2 inches of new snowfall from 1500 AKST 1/3 to 0300 AKST 1/4 during this event. An additional 3.8 inches fell after 0300 AKST 1/4 until the next midnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A gale force Low developed in the Western Gulf on Thursday, Jan. 4th. This main Low stalled in the Central Gulf while the associated strong fronts moved onto the SE Alaska Coast Thursday Night. These fronts did not significantly erode the cold air that was over the Panhandle. The result was that heavy snow occurred on Thursday Night and Friday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 2030 AKST, Thursday 1/4, Haines had 5.0 measured since morning, with it currently snowing 1 inch/hour. At 1217 AKST 1/5, a trained spotter had measured 26.0 inches for 24 hours. Another spotter in Haines had 15.0 inches for the same time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of eastward propagating triple-point Lows developed south of the Gulf of Alaska on Saturday Afternoon, 1/13. The eastern-most of these Lows moved up into the Central Gulf Saturday Night. A strong warm-front preceeding this system moved into the Northern Panhandle for significant heavy snow in the Haines area Saturday Night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Haines Customs measured 3.0 inches new snowfall for 24 hours ending 0700 AKST 1/14 and 5.5 inches new snowfall for 24 hours ending 0700 AKST 1/15. |Analyses indicate that the storm total of 8.5 inches occurred between midnight and noon on 1/14.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The arctic front persisted over the Central Panhandle on Tuesday, January 23rd. A series of weak Lows over the Southern and Central Gulf of Alaska continued to push warm air over the arctic front.  Upslope conditions enhanced the snow in the Northern Panhandle. Cold air was trapped in the Misty Fjords near Hyder at this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hyder CO-OP measured 2.5 inches new on 1/23. At 0940 AKST 1/24 Hyder measured 12.2 inches new snow for 24 hours with continued heavy at that time. The 24 hour total on 1/25 was only 2.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm force low rapidly developed to the southwest with a very strong frontal system on the afternoon of Saturday, January 6th. The Low center made landfall near Cape Decision Saturday evening at 979 MB. Very strong surface pressure gradients were ahead of this Storm. Warm air was able to overrun along the northern quadrants of this Low for heavy snow Sunday morning. A secondary Low developed in the Central Gulf pushing more warm, moist air over the arctic front Sunday afternoon and evening, Jan. 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hyder measured 17.0 inches new snowfall for 24 hours ending at 0800 AKST on 1/7.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure center just east of Kamchatka on the 23rd began weakening while moving norhteast over Far East Russia on the 24th. On the 25th it moved across the southern Chukchi Sea reaching Barrow and on the 26th it continued east merging with a stronger low in the high Arctic, and being pushed northeast by a cold front. This front increased winds over the Eastern Beaufort Sea Coast as well as brough a fresh snowfall, likely creating blizzard conditions at Kaktovik on the 26th, as indicated by the Barter Island AWOS, as well as brief high winds reported by the AWOS with the highest gust to 57 knots (66 mph).
A 975 mb low moved north over the central Bering Sea on the night of the 2nd and during the 3rd, creating strong winds at Red Dog Mine, where the maximum reported gust was 66 knots (76 mph).

A trained spotter measured 16.0 inches of new snowfall for 12 hours ending at 0600 2/22. Zone 26 was buried.

A thunderstorm produced very large hail in the Delta Junction area.  A picture of the hailstones appeared in the online version of the local weekly paper the Delta Wind. Hail diameter was estimated from the picture. Unofficial reports noted the hail was golf-ball size. Property off Nistler Road in the Clearwater area suffered some damage, the residents estimated hailstones of ping-pong ball size. At this property, the hail made numerous dents in the roofs and siding (all metal) of the home and garage. In addition, an outdoor night light was shattered. Damage costs were not provided by the owner, but is estimated to be $15,000.

A weak upper level low moved slowly east over the Yukon-Tanana Uplands on the 7th and 8th. Up to 3 of rain fell across the region over a 36 hour period. ||Zone 223: The gage on the Goodpaster River does not have a very long period of record (8yrs). Flood stage has not been defined for this river so begin/end times of flooding has been estimated. Flooding occurred in June 2006 when the river reached a stage of 20.0' and the Goodpaster climbed to a new record stage of 20.24' at 12am AST on June 8th, 2007. During the 2006 event several cabins were surrounded by water and there was a significant amount of bank erosion reported. Therefore based on past records a stage of 20.24' would have resulted in significant overbank flooding. The USGS took a flow measurement the afternoon of June 8th, about 12 hrs after the crest, and reported that the river was flowing at bankfull levels (stage was 18.5'). No damage has been reported. ||Zone 224: Fortymile River: The stage of 88.71' is the highest level on the Fortymile since 2001. Flood stage has not been defined for this river and the begin/end times of flooding have been estimated. Even at a high stages nothing is impacted near the gage site. The water was well up into the trees and it is assumed that a significant amount of water was flowing overbank in stretches along the river that are not as steep as the vicinity of the gage. No damage has been reported to NWS. Suction dredge miners and campers were likely the only people impacted in this remote drainage.

No damage has been reported to NWS. Suction dredge miners and campers were likely the only people impacted in this remote drainage.

A 996MB Low was centered near the southern tip of the Kenai Peninsula. This storm generated gale force winds across the northern gulf of Alaska and warning level winds over portions of the northern gulf coast through Portage pass and along Turnagain Arm.
An approaching front combined with a building ridge of high pressure along the Gulf Coast of Alaska produced the typical strong gap wind along Turnagain Arm.

CZ_NAME: DIXON ENTRANCE TO CAPE DECISION COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 964 MB hurricane force Low in the Western Gulf of Alaska on Sunday 11/25 slowly weakened to 971 MB by early Monday morning 11/26. A strong front moved onto Prince of Wales Island late Sunday night on 11/25.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP Observer in Craig (CFSA2) recorded a wind gust of 77 MPH on the evening of Sunday 11/25.

CZ_NAME: SALISBURY SOUND TO CAPE FAIRWEATHER COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first storm force Low approaching SE Alaska took a right turn and missed the area on 10/20. On 10/21 at 0300 AKST a second storm force low had developed to 974 MB just off the Queen Charlotte Islands. This storm center gradually weakened as it moved northward along the Gulf Coast filling to 986 MB just off Cape Spencer Sunday afternoon 10/21. The storm rapidly dissipated over the Central Gulf on Sunday night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NCEP surface analyses on 10/21 showed extreme surface pressure gradients associated with the second Low as it moved off Cape Spencer. These indicated wind gusts in excess of 60 MPH, but no observations confirmed this speed.

CZ_NAME: CAPE FAIRWEATHER TO CAPE SUCKLING COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early Friday morning on October 5th, a secondary gale force Low developed to 983 MB near Middleton Island. That afternoon a strong front moved onshore in the Eastern Gulf of Alaska causing strong, gusty winds. The Low  center moved over Yakutat at 982 MB late that afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 6:30 AM AKST on 10/5, several roofs were blown off of cabins at the Situk River Fish Camp with wind gusts of at least 65 mph estimated. Minor roof damage was done in Yakutat City (tar paper blown off  a roof).

CZ_NAME: DIXON ENTRANCE TO CAPE DECISION COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 972 MB gale force Low over the Queen Charlotte Islands on Friday night 11/9 deepened to storm force 967 MB just off Sitka early Saturday 11/10. This storm center then rapidly weakened to 976 MB just off Cape Spenser by mid Saturday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Surface pressure gradients from the NCEP analysis at 0300 AKST 11/10 were extreme across Prince of Wales Island. After the storm center moved north of the area, a marine observation at Cape Decision (CDEA2) had a peak wind of 72 KT at 1000 AKST. Other observations that Saturday further inland also showed high wind.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NWS ASOS at Ketchikan Airport (PAKT) observed a gust to 55 Knots at 0453 AKST on 11/10. The were many peak winds recorded that day at 48 KT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm moved across Kodiak Island December 24th to the northern Gulf of Alaska. Snow fell in advance of the low across Kodiak island spreading to the southern Kenai Peninsula. Strong wind associated with this storm combined with the snow to produce blizzard conditions across lake Iliamna followed by blizzard conditions across Kodiak island. Heavy snow fell over the southern Kenai Peninsula to Portage pass. The strong wind hit those areas producing blizzard conditions in the pass and along Turnagain Arm.

This is the case of a very strong, long lasting Fall storm in the Gulf of Alaska. On Wed. afternoon, 11/14 a Low developed to 980 MB off the Queen Charlotte Islands. This system weakened rapidly as another developed well to the SW. By Thu afternoon 11/15, the second Low had become storm force to 952 MB, again, off the Queen Charlottes, but with a strong occluded front approaching Dixon Entrance. That night, the Low deepened to Hurricane Force 950 MB and stalled off Cape Muzon barely weakening into Friday 11/16. The storm lasted into Friday night, and finally weakened to 973 MB in the Central Gulf on Saturday, 11/17.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hydaburg ASOS (PAHY) recorded a gust to 52 KT at 2136 AKST on 11/15. Very windy conditions in the 40 to 50 MPH range persisted around the Southern Inland Channels (Zone 28) on 10/15-17. A marine C-MAN observation at Cape Decision (CDEA2) observed a peak wind 71 KT at 0300 AKST 11/16.

A trough of low pressure extended from the Gulf of Alaska into the Susitna  Valley. Moisture flowed from the Gulf over the southern Kenai Peninsula over a very cold air mass producing heavy snow over the Kachemak Bay area December 20th and 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Seldovia Airport reported 14 inches of snow overnight December 20th. A spotter along Dimond Ridge in Homer reported 8 inches of snow but indicated more snow fell but it was difficult to get an accurate measurement due to drifting.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense Bering Sea storm with its associated front into the north Pacific produced heavy rain along the Alaska Peninsula. The combination of the heavy rain and a high tide resulted in excess water flow from Chignik Lake becoming dammed in the mouth of the river and flooding along the road into town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain overnight November 30th into the morning of December 1st produced local flooding in Chignik.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ketchikan Airport ASOS (PAKT) recorded over 6 hours of below freezing temperatures starting at 1900 AKST 10/18. Thorne Bay minimum temperature on 10/19 was 26F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 996MB Low was centered near the southern tip of the Kenai Peninsula. This storm generated gale force winds across the northern gulf of Alaska and warning level winds over portions of the northern gulf coast through Portage pass and along Turnagain Arm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain and winds began across the area on the morning of October 25th. The Portage ASOS recorded periods of heavy rain and winds gusting in the upper teens to upper 20's during the morning hours...with gusts reaching into the upper 40's by afternoon. Winds and rain continued across the zone with gusts increasing through late afternoon. At 5:53 PM,  a peak wind of 73KT registered on the ASOS. Winds gradually diminished thereafter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low in the northern Gulf of Alaska pushed warm air over the cold air trapped in the Copper River Basin. This produced localized freezing rain that accumulated 2 inches of ice in the Eureka area.

CZ_NAME: DIXON ENTRANCE TO CAPE DECISION COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Storm force Low developed to 990 MB just off Sitka Thursday 9/20 late that night. The Low further developed to 984 MB over Juneau at 984 MB at 3 AM AKST Friday 9/21 with a very strong front extending through the Panhandle. This storm rapidly dissipated midday Friday as the center moved into the Coast Mountains to the East of the Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cape Decision (CDEA2) had observations of sustained West wind greater than 60 MPH from 0100 AKST Friday 9/12 to 0700 AKST that morning. Lincoln Rock (LCNA2) had a 70 MPH measured sustained wind at 0100 AKST 9/21 and peak winds as high as 98 MPH up to 0600 AKST. The peak wind at 0600 AKST was 60 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low in the Gulf of Alaska had strong northwest flow around the west side of the storm. This produced the typical strong gap wind through the mountainous terrain of the Alaska Peninsula.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The weather front from a large storm in the Bering Sea moved north over western Alaska and brought east to southeast winds of 26 knots (30 mph) to 39 knots (45 mph) near Deering on the night of the 18th. This created a combination of locally high sea level and ocean waves which flooded the road between the village of Deering and the Deering airport during the night of the 18th. Damage amounts are unknown but expected to be minimal.

Clear skies, light winds, and a nearby arctic airmass to the north on the night of 10/4 combined to cause the first hard freeze of the season for some of the northern Zones in Southeast Alaska. ||A Hard Freeze in Alaska is defined as freezing temperatures for six hours or more or the temperature reaching 28F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gustavus Airport (PAGS AWOS) observed a minimum temperature of 25F on the morning of Thursday, 10/4 at 0136 AKST. Freezing temperatures persisted for almost 8 hours during the nighttime and early morning hours.

CZ_NAME: DIXON ENTRANCE TO CAPE DECISION COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hurricane Force Low moved into the Western Gulf on 10/30. This system caused a series of strong fronts moving onto the Gulf Coast 10/30 and 10/31, with a secondary 987 MB Low developing and moving into the Northern Panhandle early Wednesday morning 10/31.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NWS CO-OP Observer in Craig Alaska estimated wind gusts over 70 MPH and a peak wind of 80 MPH between 0100 and 0330 AKST 10/31.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weather front moved north from the Gulf of Alaska creating strong southerly winds over the Alaska Range. The Antler Creek Road Weather Information System (RWIS) reported a gust to 63 knots (73 mph). The weather front weakened rapidly on the night of the 25th.

CZ_NAME: DIXON ENTRANCE TO CAPE DECISION COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Hurricane Force Bering Sea Low generated a strong frontal system that moved into the Central Gulf on 10/26.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NWS CO-OP Observer in Craig, Alaska estimated wind gusts 65 MPH between 0800 AKST 10/26 and 0000 AKST 10/27.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong 966MB surface low moved into the western Gulf of Alaska and positioned itself just to the west of Kodiak Island. The surface gradients were oriented in a Northwest to Southeast manner which provided maximum funneling of winds through the Chugach Mountains of western Prince William Sound. The typical gap win through Portage Pass produce gusts to 90 mph in Portage Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds were generated in conjunction with the strong surface low and channeled terrain of western Prince William sound for the community of Whittier and in Portage Valley. Winds first reached 75 mph at 219 PM in Portage Valley and frequently gusted to 75 mph or greater through 1 AM November 21st. Whittier reported wind gusts to 72 mph during this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low moved across the Alaska Peninsula then north along the Bering Sea coast to the Kuskokwim Delta the evening of December 7th. The tight pressure gradient with rapidly rising pressure over the north Gulf Coast produced a brief period of the typically strong cross mountain and gap wind over the south central region of Alaska.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Cooperative Observer at Galena reported that during the morning of October 9th, six inches of snow fell with another inch falling during the evening.  A storm total of 8 inches of new snow had fallen by the morning of October 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: According to Galena Co-Op observer at KIYU Radio, Galena received 6 inches from roughly 9 AM to 12 Noon Local Time (17Z-20Z UTC).  Another inch fell into the evening and another inch by the morning of Oct 10th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 970 mb low in the central Bering Sea was stationary on the 11th and 12th and produced tides about 3 feet above normal and southeast winds to 30 mph with waves 6 to 8 feet over Norton Sound. The State of Alaska Department of Transportation reported that Mile 22 on the road from Nome to Council was washed out, with water overflowing at mile 23 and 24. The DOT reported the cost to repair the road at $12,063.50.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Alaska DOT reported washout of portion of the road at mile post 22 on Nome-Council road and water overflowing the road between mile 23 and 24 on the same road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low in the central Bering Sea earlier in the week began to weaken but move north to Saint Lawrence Island. This created southeast to south winds around 26 knots (30 mph) with gusts as high as 40 knots (46 mph) resulting in strong wave action and shore erosion on the ocean side of the village of Kivalina. The sea wall was breached and damaged in 3 places by this event. No other damage to structures occurred. Damage amount not available. 280 residents were evacuated from the village on the evening of the 12th; they were returned to the village on the 14th. The remaining 50 villagers who remained reinforced the damaged seawall and a newly eroded section of shoreline north of the seawall. This event put at risk a set of fuel tanks holding 233,710 gallons of diesel fuel for the village.

A strong low moved up to the Alaska Peninsula the night of December 25th producing very strong wind across the peninsula. Wind gusts reached 75 mph in Cold Bay damaging buildings and roofs in Cold Bay and King Cove.
A storm moved to the Aleutians producing strong wind and snow that resulted in a blizzard across portions of the central Aleutians.

A storm in the Gulf of Alaska brought snow and wind to Northern Prince William Sound in Thompson Pass to Keystone Canyon and in Portage Valley. Heavy snow fell across the region with 17 inches of snow reported in Thompson Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observed on RWIS cameras and instruments at 12 mile and 25 mile of the Richardson Highway. Also, DOT employees reported severely reduced visibility with blowing snow and wind gusts to 34 kts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm in the Gulf of Alaska brought snow and wind to Northern Prince William Sound in Thompson Pass to Keystone Canyon and in Portage Valley. Heavy snow fell across the region with 17 inches of snow reported in Thompson Pass.
A strong, cold high pressure center was positioned over the Interior and strong low pressure was centered over the northeastern Gulf of Alaska.
